channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Meta Quest 3 and 3S Price Hikes Coming in Just a Few Days
item.content:encoded:
Meta will be raising the price of its Quest 3 and 3S VR headsets later this week, it's been revealed, in a move that puts the tech giant in line with a number of other industry bodies.

As spotted by UploadVR, the price hikes will be coming into effect on Sunday (April 19th), with Meta blaming rising costs for "high-performance VR hardware". The company goes on to say that a surge in the price for critical components is hitting every sector of the industry, not just VR.

It's hard to disagree with that; last month, Sony hiked the PS5's prices due to "pressures" in the economy, and while the Japanese gaming giant didn't explicitly point to the cost of memory and other components, that's almost certainly the culprit.

These price hikes aren't insignificant, either. Here's what the Meta Quest 3 and 3S will cost you after the prices go up.

  • Meta Quest 3 (512GB) - $600 (was $500)
  • Meta Quest 3S (128GB) - $350 (was $300)
  • Meta Quest 3S (256GB) - $450 (was $400)

As UploadVR notes, the Quest 3 will still technically be cheaper than it was at launch, since the headset would have set you back $650 in 2023.

However, the entry-level Quest 3S devices are a different story; this is just a straightforward price increase for those, and one that will make them a harder prospect for those looking to enter the VR market.

There's one very small silver lining here. Meta Quest accessories apparently won't be going up in price at all, although that's likely because they don't require any memory to manufacture. Still, it's nice to know you won't be paying through the nose for extras.

If you'd like to grab a Meta Quest 3 or 3S VR headset, you can do so via Meta's official Quest website. I'd recommend doing so soon if you want to avoid the price increases.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph
